vignette: A 25-year-old male with Type 1 DM presents with nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain for 24 hours. He ran out of insulin 2 days ago. Vitals: BP 90/60, HR 120, RR 30. Labs: Glucose 480 mg/dL, pH 7.1, HCO3 8 mEq/L, Anion gap 24, K+ 5.8 mEq/L. Urine ketones positive. What is the FIRST step in management?

explanation.whyCorrect: DKA management: 1st = IV FLUIDS (NS 1-2L bolus). Insulin started AFTER K+ checked (>3.3) and fluids begun.

whyWrong.A: Insulin bolus is NOT recommended.
whyWrong.C: Bicarbonate is controversial, reserved for pH <6.9.
whyWrong.D: K+ 5.8 does not require calcium (reserved for >6.5 or ECG changes).
whyWrong.E: Insulin infusion is step 2.

explanation.clinicalPearl: DKA: 1) Fluids 2) Check K+ (>3.3 before insulin!) 3) Insulin infusion 4) D5 when glucose <200.

vignette: A 32-year-old female presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, and tremor for 2 months. She has a diffuse, non-tender goiter. Labs: TSH <0.01, Free T4 elevated. TSI positive. What is the BEST initial treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Graves disease. First-line: Methimazole + Propranolol. PTU is reserved for 1st trimester pregnancy.

whyWrong.A: RAI is second-line if methimazole fails.
whyWrong.D: PTU is second-line (hepatotoxicity risk).
whyWrong.E: Levothyroxine treats HYPOthyroidism.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Graves: Methimazole (NOT PTU) + Propranolol. PTU only in 1st trimester.

vignette: A 50-year-old male with rheumatoid arthritis on chronic prednisone (10mg daily for 2 years) undergoes emergency surgery. Post-operatively, he develops hypotension refractory to fluids, hyponatremia (Na 128), and hyperkalemia (K+ 5.9). What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: Adrenal crisis due to secondary adrenal insufficiency

explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic steroids suppress HPA axis → adrenal atrophy → adrenal crisis during stress. Treatment: IV hydrocortisone 100mg STAT.

whyWrong.A: Sepsis would not explain the classic electrolyte pattern.
whyWrong.C: AKI alone would not cause this pattern.
whyWrong.D: SIADH causes euvolemic hyponatremia without hyperkalemia.
whyWrong.E: Primary AI would have hyperpigmentation.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Chronic steroids = Stress-dose steroids before surgery! Hydrocortisone 50mg q8h or 100mg IV STAT.

vignette: A 55-year-old female has hypercalcemia on routine labs (Ca 11.2 mg/dL). She is asymptomatic. PTH is elevated (120 pg/mL). 24-hour urinary calcium is elevated. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Hypercalcemia + elevated PTH = Primary hyperparathyroidism. Elevated urinary calcium rules out FHH.

whyWrong.B: Secondary hyperparathyroidism has LOW or normal calcium.
whyWrong.C: FHH has low 24h urinary calcium.
whyWrong.D: Malignancy causes PTH-independent hypercalcemia (low PTH).
whyWrong.E: Vitamin D toxicity causes hypercalcemia with LOW PTH.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hypercalcemia: High PTH = Primary hyperparathyroidism. Low PTH = Malignancy, Vitamin D toxicity.

vignette: A 40-year-old female presents with amenorrhea, galactorrhea, and headaches for 6 months. Prolactin is 250 ng/mL. MRI shows 1.2cm pituitary adenoma compressing the optic chiasm. Visual field testing shows bitemporal hemianopsia. What is the BEST treatment?

explanation.whyCorrect: Macroprolactinoma + visual field deficit = surgical emergency. Transsphenoidal surgery first.

whyWrong.A: Cabergoline is first-line for MOST prolactinomas, but visual deficits require urgent surgery.
whyWrong.C: Bromocriptine is less effective than cabergoline.
whyWrong.D: Radiation is adjunctive.
whyWrong.E: Macroprolactinoma + visual symptoms requires treatment.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Prolactinoma + Visual deficit = Surgery first. Without visual deficit = Cabergoline.

vignette: A 70-year-old female with osteoporosis (T-score -3.2 at hip) is started on alendronate 70mg weekly. After 1 year, she fractures her wrist after a fall. DEXA shows T-score -3.3 (stable). What is the BEST next step?

options.text: Switch to denosumab 60mg SC every 6 months
Switch to IV zoledronic acid 5mg annually

explanation.whyCorrect: Fracture on bisphosphonate = Treatment failure. Anabolic therapy (teriparatide, romosozumab) is indicated.

whyWrong.A: Fracture on therapy = do not continue same drug.
whyWrong.D: Raloxifene is weaker antiresorptive.
whyWrong.E: Zoledronic acid is same class (bisphosphonate).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Fracture on bisphosphonate = Anabolic therapy. Do NOT switch between antiresorptives.

vignette: A 35-year-old female is found to have a 2.5cm solitary thyroid nodule. She has no compressive symptoms. TSH is normal. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Thyroid nodule >1cm + normal TSH: First step = ultrasound to risk-stratify (TI-RADS). FNA based on US findings.

whyWrong.B: FNA is based on ultrasound risk stratification.
whyWrong.C: Surgery without prior FNA is inappropriate.
whyWrong.D: CT is not the imaging of choice.
whyWrong.E: Thyroid scan is for LOW TSH (hyperthyroidism).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Thyroid nodule + normal/high TSH → US first → FNA based on TI-RADS.

vignette: A 30-year-old male presents with polyuria (8L/day), polydipsia, and nocturia. Serum Na 150, serum osmolality 310, urine osmolality 150. After water deprivation test, urine osmolality increases to 750 after desmopressin. What type of DI?

explanation.whyCorrect: Central DI: Hypernatremia + dilute urine (low urine osmolality despite high serum osmolality). Responds to desmopressin (urine osmolality increases >50% or >750). Caused by ADH deficiency. Treatment: Desmopressin (DDAVP).

whyWrong.B: Nephrogenic DI: No response to desmopressin (kidneys resistant to ADH).
whyWrong.C: Primary polydipsia: Low-normal serum Na, low serum osmolality, urine concentrates appropriately.
whyWrong.D: Gestational DI: Rare, due to increased vasopressinase from placenta.
whyWrong.E: SIADH: Hyponatremia (Na 150 is hypernatremia).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Central DI: Responds to desmopressin. Nephrogenic DI: No response. Water deprivation test + desmopressin = Gold standard.

vignette: A 42-year-old female with BMI 38 and type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.5%) has failed lifestyle modifications and metformin. She wants to lose weight and improve glycemic control. Which medication would BEST address both goals?

explanation.whyCorrect: Semaglutide (GLP-1 RA) at 2.4mg weekly: Approved for obesity (BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidities). Provides significant weight loss (15% body weight) + excellent glycemic control. Liraglutide 3mg is alternative.

whyWrong.A: Insulin causes weight GAIN, not weight loss.
whyWrong.C: DPP4 inhibitors are weight-neutral, minimal A1c reduction.
whyWrong.D: Pioglitazone causes weight gain and fluid retention.
whyWrong.E: Sulfonylureas cause weight gain and hypoglycemia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Obesity + DM: GLP-1 RAs (semaglutide, liraglutide) or Tirzepatide (GIP/GLP-1). Weight loss + Glycemic control.

vignette: A 50-year-old female without diabetes presents with episodes of confusion, sweating, and palpitations that resolve with eating. Fasting glucose during an episode is 40 mg/dL, insulin level is 10 μU/mL (inappropriately elevated), C-peptide is elevated. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Insulinoma: Fasting hypoglycemia + Inappropriately elevated insulin + Elevated C-peptide (endogenous insulin). Whipple triad: Symptoms of hypoglycemia + Low glucose + Relief with glucose. Treatment: Surgical resection.

whyWrong.B: Exogenous insulin: Elevated insulin but LOW C-peptide (exogenous insulin suppresses endogenous production).
whyWrong.C: Adrenal insufficiency causes hypoglycemia with LOW insulin (not elevated).
whyWrong.D: NICTH: Low glucose + Low insulin + High IGF-2 (from large tumors).
whyWrong.E: Dumping syndrome: Postprandial hypoglycemia (reactive), not fasting.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Insulinoma: Fasting hypoglycemia + High insulin + High C-peptide. Exogenous insulin: High insulin + LOW C-peptide.

vignette: A 40-year-old male has eruptive xanthomas on his back, TG 2500 mg/dL, total cholesterol 400. He has no history of diabetes. What is the MOST serious immediate risk?

explanation.whyCorrect: Severe hypertriglyceridemia (TG >1000, especially >2000): Acute pancreatitis is the most immediate life-threatening risk. TG >2000 increases risk dramatically. Treatment: Fibrates, high-dose omega-3 fatty acids, strict diet, avoid alcohol, control diabetes if present.

whyWrong.A: CAD is a long-term risk but pancreatitis is the immediate concern.
whyWrong.C: Stroke risk is elevated but not as immediately life-threatening as pancreatitis.
whyWrong.D: PAD is a chronic complication of atherosclerosis.
whyWrong.E: Aortic stenosis is unrelated to hypertriglyceridemia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: TG >1000 = Pancreatitis risk. TG >2000 = Emergency! Treat with fibrates, omega-3, and strict dietary fat restriction.

vignette: A 55-year-old obese male (BMI 36) with type 2 diabetes has HbA1c 8.5% despite metformin 2g and empagliflozin 25mg. He needs additional glucose control and significant weight loss. What is the BEST add-on therapy per ADA 2024?

explanation.whyCorrect: Tirzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist. SURPASS trials showed superior HbA1c reduction (2.0-2.4%) and weight loss (10-15%) vs semaglutide, insulin, and other comparators. ADA 2024 recommends GLP-1 RA or dual GIP/GLP-1 as preferred injectable before insulin for patients needing additional glucose control with weight benefit. Also has emerging CV benefit.

whyWrong.A: Insulin causes weight gain and hypoglycemia; reserved after non-insulin options fail.
whyWrong.B: Tirzepatide: Superior HbA1c + Weight loss vs all comparators in SURPASS program.
whyWrong.C: DPP4i have modest A1c reduction (0.5-0.7%) and no weight benefit.
whyWrong.D: Sulfonylureas cause weight gain and hypoglycemia.
whyWrong.E: Pioglitazone causes weight gain and fluid retention; less CV benefit than GLP-1 RA.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1): Superior A1c reduction + Weight loss. SURMOUNT-1: 22.5% weight loss in obesity without diabetes.

vignette: A 65-year-old male with CKD stage 3 (eGFR 35, UACR 600 mg/g) due to hypertensive nephrosclerosis does NOT have diabetes. He is on lisinopril 20mg. What medication should be added to slow CKD progression per DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY?

explanation.whyCorrect: SGLT2i are now indicated for CKD with proteinuria REGARDLESS of diabetes status. DAPA-CKD trial: Dapagliflozin reduced CKD progression, ESKD, and CV death by 39% in patients with eGFR 25-75 and UACR 200-5000, including those WITHOUT diabetes. EMPA-KIDNEY confirmed benefit in broader population. Now KDIGO 2024 Class IA recommendation.

whyWrong.A: Metformin is for diabetes; no benefit in non-diabetic CKD.
whyWrong.C: Finerenone is for diabetic CKD (FIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD); not approved for non-diabetic CKD.
whyWrong.D: ARB is already given (lisinopril is ACEi); adding ARB increases hyperkalemia risk.
whyWrong.E: Statin is for CV risk reduction, not CKD progression.

explanation.clinicalPearl: SGLT2i for CKD WITHOUT diabetes: DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY trials. KDIGO 2024: Class IA for all CKD with proteinuria regardless of diabetes.

vignette: A 45-year-old female has TSH 8.5 mIU/L (normal 0.5-4.5) and normal Free T4 on routine labs. She is asymptomatic and not pregnant. TPO antibodies are negative. What is the BEST management per ATA 2023?

options.text: Observation and repeat TSH in 6-12 months

explanation.whyCorrect: Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4.5-10, normal FT4) in asymptomatic, non-pregnant adult: OBSERVATION without treatment is appropriate. ATA 2023: Treat only if TSH >10, symptomatic, pregnant (or planning pregnancy), goiter, or TPO antibody positive with desire for treatment. Spontaneous normalization occurs in 30-40% within 2 years.

whyWrong.A: Overtreatment with 50mcg may cause iatrogenic hyperthyroidism (atrial fibrillation, osteoporosis).
whyWrong.B: Observation recommended for TSH <10 without symptoms or risk factors.
whyWrong.C: 25mcg may be appropriate if treating, but observation is preferred.
whyWrong.D: RAI is for hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism.
whyWrong.E: Thyroid US is for nodules or goiter, not subclinical hypothyroidism.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH <10): Observe unless pregnant, symptomatic, or TPO+. 30-40% normalize spontaneously.

vignette: A 72-year-old female with severe postmenopausal osteoporosis (T-score -3.8 at hip, prior vertebral fracture) has been on alendronate for 5 years. Despite this, she sustained a new vertebral fracture. What is the BEST next therapy?

options.text: Switch to romosozumab (Evenity) 210mg SC monthly x12 months
Switch to denosumab (Prolia) 60mg SC q6 months

explanation.whyCorrect: Fracture on bisphosphonate = Treatment failure. Switch to ANABOLIC therapy: Romosozumab (sclerostin inhibitor) is the most potent bone-building agent. ARCH trial showed romosozumab reduced vertebral fractures by 73% vs alendronate. Romosozumab x12 months followed by antiresorptive (denosumab or bisphosphonate). Teriparatide is alternative anabolic. Denosumab is antiresorptive (same class as alendronate).

whyWrong.A: Fracture on therapy = treatment failure. Do NOT continue same drug.
whyWrong.B: Romosozumab: Anabolic + Antiresorptive (dual action). Most potent fracture reduction.
whyWrong.C: Denosumab is antiresorptive (same mechanism as alendronate); switch to different class.
whyWrong.D: Adding raloxifene is insufficient for severe osteoporosis with fracture.
whyWrong.E: Zoledronic acid is same class as alendronate (bisphosphonate).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Fracture on bisphosphonate: Switch to ANABOLIC (romosozumab or teriparatide). Romosozumab = Most potent. Then antiresorptive maintenance.

vignette: A 55-year-old female is found to have a 3cm left adrenal mass on CT done for abdominal pain. She has hypertension but no other symptoms. What is the MOST important initial test?

options.text: Plasma metanephrines or 24h urine metanephrines to rule out pheochromocytoma

explanation.whyCorrect: Adrenal incidentaloma: ALWAYS rule out pheochromocytoma FIRST (before biopsy or surgery which can precipitate hypertensive crisis). Screen with plasma free metanephrines or 24h urine metanephrines. Also evaluate: Cushing syndrome (1mg dex suppression test), primary aldosteronism (aldosterone/renin ratio if hypertensive). Biopsy is LAST resort (only if suspected metastasis).

whyWrong.A: Biopsy can precipitate hypertensive crisis if pheochromocytoma. Must rule out biochemically first.
whyWrong.C: Surgery without biochemical workup is dangerous.
whyWrong.D: PET-CT is for malignancy workup after biochemical evaluation.
whyWrong.E: CT follow-up depends on size and imaging features (>4cm or suspicious features need sooner workup).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Adrenal incidentaloma: 1. Rule out PHEO (plasma metanephrines) 2. Cushing (dex suppression) 3. Aldosterone (if HTN). Biopsy LAST.

vignette: A 12-year-old boy has a family history of MEN2A (RET proto-oncogene mutation confirmed in mother). Genetic testing shows he carries the same RET mutation. What is the recommended management?

options.text: Annual calcitonin screening starting at age 20
Prophylactic total thyroidectomy before age 5-6 (timing based on RET codon)

explanation.whyCorrect: MEN2A with confirmed RET mutation: PROPHYLACTIC TOTAL THYROIDECTOMY. Timing depends on RET codon risk level: Highest risk (M918T): First year of life. High risk (C634): Before age 5. Moderate risk: Before age 5-10. This prevents medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) which is the leading cause of death in MEN2. Annual pheochromocytoma screening also needed.

whyWrong.A: Screening is insufficient; prophylactic thyroidectomy is standard of care.
whyWrong.B: Prophylactic thyroidectomy prevents MTC (near 100% penetrance in MEN2).
whyWrong.C: Observation allows MTC to develop (100% penetrance).
whyWrong.D: RAI is for differentiated thyroid cancer, not MTC (C-cells do not take up iodine).
whyWrong.E: Levothyroxine is replacement after thyroidectomy, not preventive.

explanation.clinicalPearl: MEN2 RET mutation: PROPHYLACTIC thyroidectomy. Timing based on codon risk. Prevents MTC (100% penetrance).

vignette: A 14-year-old female with Type 1 DM has HbA1c 9.0% despite multiple daily injections and CGM. She has frequent nocturnal hypoglycemia and dawn phenomenon. What is the BEST technology option per ADA 2024?

options.text: Continue MDI + CGM with more education
Automated insulin delivery (AID) system (hybrid closed-loop pump)

explanation.whyCorrect: AID systems (hybrid closed-loop) combine CGM + insulin pump + algorithm that automatically adjusts basal insulin delivery. Reduces HbA1c by 0.5-1.0%, increases Time-in-Range by 10-15%, and reduces hypoglycemia (especially nocturnal). ADA 2024: AID should be offered to all T1D patients who can use it safely. Ideal for patients with frequent hypoglycemia and glycemic variability.

whyWrong.A: MDI + CGM alone insufficient with HbA1c 9% and frequent hypoglycemia.
whyWrong.B: AID systems improve outcomes: More TIR, less hypoglycemia, better HbA1c.
whyWrong.C: Pump without CGM lacks automated features and hypoglycemia protection.
whyWrong.D: Premixed insulin provides less flexibility and worse glycemic control.
whyWrong.E: Islet transplant is for severe hypoglycemia unawareness refractory to technology.

explanation.clinicalPearl: T1D with hypoglycemia + high A1c: AID (hybrid closed-loop) system. Improves TIR + Reduces hypoglycemia. ADA 2024 recommends for all eligible.

vignette: A 65-year-old female with osteoporosis has 25-hydroxyvitamin D level of 15 ng/mL. She is on calcium carbonate 1200mg daily. What is the BEST vitamin D replacement regimen per Endocrine Society 2024?

options.text: Vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) 50,000 IU weekly x8 weeks, then maintenance 1000-2000 IU daily
Vitamin D3 50,000 IU daily for 1 week

explanation.whyCorrect: Vitamin D deficiency (<20 ng/mL): Repletion with Vitamin D2 or D3 50,000 IU weekly x8 weeks, then maintenance 1000-2000 IU daily. D3 (cholecalciferol) may be slightly more potent than D2. Recheck 25(OH)D after 8-12 weeks. Target >30 ng/mL for osteoporosis patients. Calcitriol is active form for CKD/hypoparathyroidism, not routine replacement.

whyWrong.A: Standard repletion: 50,000 IU weekly x8 weeks, then daily maintenance.
whyWrong.B: 1000 IU daily is maintenance dose, not repletion for deficiency.
whyWrong.C: Calcitriol is for CKD stage 4-5 or hypoparathyroidism; not routine replacement.
whyWrong.D: Deficiency with osteoporosis requires treatment to optimize bone health.
whyWrong.E: 50,000 IU daily x1 week is insufficient for repletion.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Vitamin D deficiency: Replete 50,000 IU weekly x8 weeks → Maintenance 1000-2000 IU daily. Target >30 ng/mL for osteoporosis.

vignette: A 60-year-old male with diabetes and ASCVD has LDL-C 90 on high-intensity atorvastatin. He had statin intolerance to 3 different statins. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Statin intolerance + ASCVD + LDL above goal: Add ezetimibe FIRST (IMPROVE-IT trial). If still above goal, add bempedoic acid (CLEAR Outcomes: 13% MACE reduction in statin-intolerant). PCSK9i are third-line due to cost and prior authorization requirements. Inclisiran is alternative to PCSK9i (twice-yearly injection). Fenofibrate for TG, not LDL.

whyWrong.A: Ezetimibe + Bempedoic acid: Oral, effective, step-wise approach.
whyWrong.B: PCSK9i are highly effective but reserved after ezetimibe ± bempedoic acid (cost, access).
whyWrong.C: Inclisiran is alternative PCSK9-lowering therapy (siRNA).
whyWrong.D: Fenofibrate lowers TG, not LDL.
whyWrong.E: Patient is statin-intolerant; can't tolerate statins.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Statin intolerance: Ezetimibe → Bempedoic acid → PCSK9i/Inclisiran. Bempedoic acid: Liver-specific prodrug (no muscle symptoms).

vignette: A 40-year-old female with BMI 38 without diabetes has failed lifestyle modification. She wants pharmacotherapy for weight loss. Which medication has the HIGHEST weight loss efficacy per STEP and SURMOUNT trials?

explanation.whyCorrect: Tirzepatide (Zepbound) 15mg has the HIGHEST weight loss: SURMOUNT-1 trial showed 22.5% total body weight loss at 72 weeks. Semaglutide (Wegovy) 2.4mg: 14.9% in STEP-1. Phentermine/Topiramate: 10-12%. Naltrexone/Bupropion: 5-6%. Orlistat: 3-5%. Both tirzepatide and semaglutide are FDA-approved for obesity without diabetes.

whyWrong.A: Orlistat has lowest efficacy (3-5%) and GI side effects.
whyWrong.B: Semaglutide 2.4mg is effective (14.9%) but less than tirzepatide.
whyWrong.C: Tirzepatide 15mg: 22.5% weight loss - highest efficacy to date.
whyWrong.D: Qsymia has 10-12% weight loss; teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy).
whyWrong.E: Contrave has 5-6% weight loss; contraindicated in uncontrolled HTN, seizure disorder.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Obesity pharmacotherapy (2024): Tirzepatide (22.5%) > Semaglutide (14.9%) > Phentermine/Topiramate > Naltrexone/Bupropion > Orlistat.

vignette: A 45-year-old female with Graves disease on methimazole presents with fever 40°C, tachycardia 180 bpm, confusion, and jaundice. She missed her medications for 2 weeks due to illness. What is the FIRST step in management?

options.text: PTU 500-1000mg load then 250mg q4h + IV propranolol + Hydrocortisone 100mg IV

explanation.whyCorrect: Thyroid storm (Burch-Wartofsky score ≥45): 1. PTU (blocks synthesis AND T4→T3 conversion), 2. Propranolol (blocks adrenergic symptoms, reduces T4→T3 conversion), 3. Hydrocortisone (blocks T4→T3 conversion, treats relative adrenal insufficiency). Lugol's iodine AFTER PTU (blocks release). Supportive: Cooling, fluids, treat precipitant.

whyWrong.A: Propranolol alone insufficient; must block synthesis and release.
whyWrong.C: RAI is contraindicated in thyroid storm (worsens hyperthyroidism initially).
whyWrong.D: Surgery after stabilization (not acutely in storm).
whyWrong.E: Acetaminophen may worsen hepatic dysfunction; cooling blankets preferred.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Thyroid storm: PTU (blocks synthesis) + Propranolol (symptoms) + Hydrocortisone (blocks conversion) + Iodine (AFTER PTU). Burch-Wartofsky score.

vignette: A 40-year-old female with central obesity, moon face, purple striae, and hypertension has abnormal 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol 8 μg/dL, normal <1.8). What is the NEXT step?

options.text: Diagnose Cushing syndrome and start ketoconazole
24-hour urinary free cortisol OR late-night salivary cortisol x2

explanation.whyCorrect: Abnormal screening test (dex suppression) requires CONFIRMATION with a second test: 24h urinary free cortisol (x2) OR late-night salivary cortisol (x2). Need at least 2 positive tests to diagnose Cushing syndrome. After confirmation: ACTH level to determine ACTH-dependent (pituitary/ectopic) vs ACTH-independent (adrenal). Imaging follows biochemical localization.

whyWrong.A: Need confirmatory testing before diagnosing and treating Cushing syndrome.
whyWrong.B: Confirm with 24h UFC or late-night salivary cortisol before imaging.
whyWrong.C: Adrenal CT after biochemical confirmation AND ACTH level suggesting adrenal source.
whyWrong.D: Pituitary MRI after biochemical confirmation AND ACTH level suggesting pituitary source.
whyWrong.E: Surgery after complete biochemical and localization workup.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Cushing workup: Screen (dex test) → Confirm (24h UFC or salivary cortisol) → ACTH level → Imaging (pituitary MRI or adrenal CT).

vignette: A 30-year-old male with T1DM presents with DKA (pH 7.1, HCO3 8, glucose 450, K+ 5.5). After initial fluid bolus, what is the correct insulin management per ADA 2024?

options.text: IV insulin bolus 0.1 units/kg then infusion 0.1 units/kg/hr
IV insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hr WITHOUT bolus
Wait until K+ normalized before starting insulin

explanation.whyCorrect: ADA 2024: IV insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hr WITHOUT bolus. Insulin bolus is NO LONGER recommended (increases risk of hypoglycemia and cerebral edema without benefit). Start insulin AFTER K+ confirmed >3.3 mEq/L. Continue until anion gap closed. Transition to SC insulin when pH >7.3 and HCO3 >15 with overlap of IV and SC insulin for 2-4 hours.

whyWrong.A: Insulin bolus is NOT recommended in ADA 2024 guidelines.
whyWrong.C: SC insulin is for mild DKA; IV route preferred for moderate-severe (pH <7.2).
whyWrong.D: K+ 5.5 is safe to start insulin (>3.3 threshold). Insulin will lower K+.
whyWrong.E: 0.05 units/kg/hr is for low-dose protocol; 0.1 units/kg/hr is standard.

explanation.clinicalPearl: DKA 2024: IV insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/hr WITHOUT bolus. No bolus! Check K+ >3.3 before starting.

vignette: A 60-year-old female with primary hyperparathyroidism has Ca 11.0, PTH 150, eGFR 55, and T-score -1.5 at hip (osteopenia). No fractures or kidney stones. What is the recommendation per NIH 2023 criteria?

options.text: Observation with annual labs and DEXA

explanation.whyCorrect: NIH 2023 Criteria for parathyroidectomy: Age <50, Ca >1 mg/dL above normal, eGFR <60, T-score ≤-2.5 at any site OR vertebral fracture, nephrolithiasis on imaging, or 24h urine Ca >400 mg/day. This patient has eGFR 55 (<60) = Meets criteria for surgery. Surgery is CURATIVE (adenoma resection).

whyWrong.A: eGFR <60 is a surgical indication (meets NIH criteria).
whyWrong.B: Observation is for asymptomatic patients NOT meeting surgical criteria.
whyWrong.C: Cinacalcet is for secondary hyperparathyroidism or non-surgical candidates.
whyWrong.D: Bisphosphonates address bone density but not underlying hyperparathyroidism.
whyWrong.E: Calcitonin is for acute hypercalcemia, not chronic management.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Primary hyperparathyroidism surgery: Age <50, Ca >1 above normal, eGFR <60, T-score ≤-2.5, kidney stones, urine Ca >400.

vignette: A 35-year-old female with primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) on hydrocortisone 20mg AM and 10mg PM and fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily develops gastroenteritis with vomiting. What is the BEST management?

options.text: IM hydrocortisone 100mg STAT and seek emergency care
Stop all medications until vomiting resolves

explanation.whyCorrect: Adrenal crisis prevention: Vomiting = CANNOT take oral medications = Adrenal crisis imminent. Give IM hydrocortisone 100mg STAT and go to ER. Sick day rules: For fever/infection: Double oral dose x3 days. For vomiting/surgery/trauma: IM/IV hydrocortisone (cannot rely on oral). Patients should have emergency injection kit at home and wear medical alert bracelet.

whyWrong.A: Cannot absorb oral medications if vomiting.
whyWrong.B: Vomiting = cannot take oral; needs parenteral steroids.
whyWrong.D: Stopping steroids in adrenal insufficiency can be fatal.
whyWrong.E: Fludrocortisone dose is unchanged acutely; hydrocortisone is the stress steroid.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Adrenal insufficiency + Vomiting = IM hydrocortisone 100mg + ER. Cannot rely on oral. Sick day: Double dose x3 days for fever. Emergency injection kit!

vignette: A 50-year-old male with known pituitary macroadenoma presents with sudden severe headache, visual loss, and hypotension. CT shows hemorrhage into the pituitary. What is the EMERGENCY management?

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 100mg STAT + Transsphenoidal surgical decompression

explanation.whyCorrect: Pituitary apoplexy = NEUROSURGICAL EMERGENCY. Sudden hemorrhage into pituitary causes acute hypopituitarism (hypotension from adrenal crisis) + Mass effect (visual loss). 1. IV hydrocortisone (treats adrenal insufficiency), 2. Emergency transsphenoidal decompression. Steroids BEFORE surgery (prevent adrenal crisis). MRI confirms but does not delay treatment.

whyWrong.A: Observation inappropriate for acute apoplexy with visual loss and hypotension.
whyWrong.B: IV hydrocortisone (adrenal crisis) + Surgical decompression (mass effect).
whyWrong.C: Bromocriptine is for prolactinoma, not acute apoplexy.
whyWrong.D: Radiation is for residual tumor, not acute apoplexy.
whyWrong.E: DDAVP for diabetes insipidus (may develop post-op).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pituitary apoplexy: Sudden headache + Visual loss + Hypotension = IV steroids + Emergency surgery. Adrenal crisis + Mass effect.

vignette: A 65-year-old male with T2DM on insulin glargine and glimepiride presents with confusion and glucose 45 mg/dL. He is conscious and able to swallow. What is the BEST initial treatment per ADA 2024?

options.text: 15g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets or juice) - Rule of 15

explanation.whyCorrect: Conscious hypoglycemic patient able to swallow: Rule of 15 = 15g fast-acting carbs (4 glucose tablets, 4 oz juice, or 1 tbsp honey). Recheck glucose in 15 minutes. If still <70, repeat. Once >70, eat meal/snack to prevent recurrence. Glucagon for unconscious patients (unable to swallow). IV D50 for hospitalized or unconscious patients.

whyWrong.A: Glucagon is for unconscious patients or those unable to swallow.
whyWrong.C: IV D50 is for hospitalized patients or unconscious (requires IV access).
whyWrong.D: Complex carbs after glucose normalized to prevent recurrence, not initial treatment.
whyWrong.E: Octreotide is for sulfonylurea overdose, not routine hypoglycemia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hypoglycemia (conscious): Rule of 15 = 15g carbs → Recheck in 15 min. Unconscious: Glucagon IM or D50 IV.

vignette: A 55-year-old male with obesity and T2DM has low testosterone (180 ng/dL on 2 morning levels), low libido, and ED. LH is low. Prolactin is normal. He wants testosterone therapy. What must be done FIRST?

options.text: Weight loss and lifestyle modification; discuss fertility implications

explanation.whyCorrect: Functional hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low T + low/normal LH) in obese T2DM: WEIGHT LOSS is the primary treatment. Obesity causes aromatization of testosterone to estradiol in adipose tissue → suppresses HPG axis. Weight loss can increase testosterone by 200-300 ng/dL. Also discuss: Fertility (testosterone therapy suppresses spermatogenesis), CV safety (TRAVERSE trial showed non-inferior CV safety but long-term data limited).

whyWrong.A: Weight loss first; testosterone therapy may not be needed and causes infertility.
whyWrong.C: MRI if high prolactin, panhypopituitarism, or severe hypogonadism with very low T.
whyWrong.D: Clomiphene is off-label for hypogonadism; preserves fertility but less studied.
whyWrong.E: Injections if testosterone therapy truly indicated after lifestyle modification.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Low T in obesity: Weight loss FIRST (aromatase activity in fat). Testosterone therapy = INFERTILITY (discuss sperm banking if desired).

vignette: A 25-year-old male with MEN1 has primary hyperparathyroidism (Ca 11.8, PTH 200) and a 2cm pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (gastrinoma) with fasting gastrin 1100 pg/mL. What is the BEST surgical approach?

options.text: Subtotal parathyroidectomy + Distal pancreatectomy with lymph node dissection

explanation.whyCorrect: MEN1 with hyperparathyroidism and functional PanNET (gastrinoma): Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands, leave 0.5 or total with autotransplant) + Distal pancreatectomy with lymph node dissection. Gastrinomas in MEN1 are often multiple and in duodenum. PPI for symptom control. Cure is rare but debulking reduces malignant risk. Whipple only for large pancreatic head lesions.

whyWrong.A: Pancreatic NET also needs surgical management.
whyWrong.C: Total pancreatectomy is excessive (diabetes risk).
whyWrong.D: Whipple reserved for pancreatic head lesions not amenable to enucleation.
whyWrong.E: PPIs control symptoms but surgery needed for malignant potential and cure.

explanation.clinicalPearl: MEN1: Parathyroidectomy (subtotal) + PanNET surgery (distal pancreatectomy or enucleation). PPIs for gastrinoma symptoms.

vignette: A 45-year-old female with Hashimoto thyroiditis (euthyroid on levothyroxine, TPO antibodies >1000) presents with subacute confusion, seizures, and tremors. CSF shows elevated protein without pleocytosis. Anti-thyroid antibodies are elevated in CSF. What is the diagnosis and treatment?

options.text: Hashimoto encephalopathy (SREAT) - IV methylprednisolone 1g x5 days

explanation.whyCorrect: Hashimoto encephalopathy (SREAT - Steroid-Responsive Encephalopathy Associated with Autoimmune Thyroiditis): Elevated TPO/Tg antibodies + Encephalopathy + Elevated CSF protein + Dramatic response to STEROIDS. Diagnosis of exclusion (rule out infection, CJD, autoimmune encephalitis). Treatment: IV methylprednisolone 1g x3-5 days, then oral prednisone taper. Most patients recover completely.

whyWrong.A: CSF without pleocytosis argues against viral encephalitis.
whyWrong.B: SREAT: Steroid-responsive, elevated TPO, normal thyroid function.
whyWrong.C: CJD has rapid progression, DWI changes, 14-3-3 protein, no steroid response.
whyWrong.D: Anti-NMDAR has orofacial dyskinesias, ovarian teratoma, CSF oligoclonal bands.
whyWrong.E: Myxedema coma has severe hypothyroidism (not euthyroid), hypothermia, bradycardia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hashimoto encephalopathy (SREAT): High TPO antibodies + Encephalopathy + Steroid-responsive. Normal thyroid function! Steroids = Dramatic improvement.
